title: Presiding Officers
lead: ==‘The Speaker's position should be free, honoured, and represented by men of outstanding ability and impartiality — it is the symbol of the nation's freedom and liberty’== — Nehru, March 1948. Yet there was ==no Deputy Speaker for the entire 17th LS==, and none elected ‘thus far’ in the 18th.

items.title: All Presiding Officers
bullets: ==Casting vote== on equality of votes (Art 100); salary charged on the CFI
House commences only when the PO sits; adjournments, quorum suspension
Admissibility of ==motions and resolutions== (incl. no-confidence, cuts); selection of amendments; Panel of Chairs
Discipline: who speaks and for how long; ==points of order==; expunging; withdrawal of members
Executive accountability: order of business, ==regulating questions==, conducting debates
==Quasi-judicial==: anti-defection; prima facie contempt/privilege; committees for judges' removal (Judges Inquiry Act, 1968)
Rulings are ==precedents==; corrects patent errors on the floor

items.title: LS Speaker (Extra)
bullets: ==Certifies Money Bills (Art 110)==
Presides over ==joint sittings==
Supreme control of parliamentary committees
Ex-officio President of the ==Indian Parliamentary Group==
Chairs Business Advisory, Rules, General Purposes Committees

items.title: Deputy Speaker
bullets: ==Continuity of legislative work==; neutrality for important debates
Presides over House Budget & ==Private Member Bills== Committees
==No timeline== — Art 93 says only ‘as soon as’

items.title: RS Chairman (VP)
bullets: Nominates members to Press Council selection committee and to boards of ==BHU, JNU, JMI==, NCTE, Haj Committee etc.
Nominates RS members to ICCR, Central Social Welfare Board and more

blocks.text: Rationale: ==constitutional necessity, institutional neutrality, democratic accountability==. ‘The role of the Speaker is to enable the House, not shut it’ — N. Sanjiva Reddy, 1968.

items.title: Case Studies
bullets: ==N. Sanjiva Reddy== resigned from his party in 1969 while contesting for President
WB & Punjab Speakers adjourned Houses for prolonged periods (1968-69) — vacuum
==Somnath Chatterjee== ignored party direction on the Nuclear Deal (14th LS)
==Sumitra Mahajan's Aadhaar-as-Money-Bill== certification remains contentious
Hamid Ansari's 2013 Zero Hour ruling improved decorum; K.R. Narayanan's digitisation won laurels
==No-confidence moved against Speaker Om Birla (Feb 2026)==; VP Dhankhar resigned late 2025

items.title: Best Practices Abroad
bullets: ==UK==: Speaker cuts party ties and seeks re-election as an independent
==Australia==: Speaker's rulings open to discussion
==Canada==: Deputy Speakers rotate between parties
==New Zealand==: written codes of conduct; decisions reviewed

items.title: Issues
bullets: ==Partisan behaviour== — perceived ruling-party bias
==Anti-defection delays== — no timeline weakens the law
==Money Bill certification misused== to bypass the RS
Limited judicial review of final decisions
Frequent ==disruptions==, weak control

items.title: Solutions
bullets: ==Opposition-consensus election== (Halim Committee 1994; 170th Law Commission)
==Mandatory resignation from party== (UK model)
==3-month deadline== for anti-defection rulings ([[Keisham Meghachandra Singh (2020)]]); or an ==independent tribunal==
Rotation of chairing duties (Parliamentary Standing Committee, 2022)
Strengthen the Deputy Speaker — ==60-day appointment timeline==
Codify powers & procedures (Privileges Committee, LS 2008)

head: Provision
Meaning
rows: LS chooses a ==Speaker and Deputy Speaker==.
Removal by resolution passed by a ==majority of all the then members==.
Speaker ==cannot preside== while his removal is considered.
May speak and vote ==in the first instance== — but no casting vote.

items: The ==2026 motion against Speaker Om Birla== was negatived by voice vote — constitutionally a ==removal resolution==, not a no-confidence motion.

==Issues==: the absolute-majority threshold makes a majority-backed Speaker ==nearly irremovable==; the Speaker is judged by the very political House he controls; ==Rule 200A== admissibility can be a gatekeeping tool; the ==50-members-rising== requirement shuts out small parties; ==no independent fact-finding==; conflict with the Speaker's Tenth Schedule role — [[Nabam Rebia]]: a Speaker facing removal notice should not decide disqualifications (a rule itself open to tactical abuse); judicial review limited (Art 122, but [[Raja Ram Pal]] allows review for ==substantive illegality==); a vacant Deputy Speakership weakens neutral chairing.

==Solutions==: time-bound Deputy Speaker election (30–60 days); preliminary all-party ==scrutiny committee==; objective drafting standards for Rule 200A; guaranteed listing after threshold support; ==recorded voting==, not voice vote; move anti-defection to an ==independent tribunal==; stronger convention of Speaker neutrality.

blocks.text: India's second-highest office is a ==constitutional hinge==: its normal function is legislative (ex-officio ==RS Chairman==); its contingent function is executive (==substitute for the President==).

items.title: Strengths
bullets: ==Constitutional continuity== — the Presidency never falls vacant
Stable leadership for a House that ==never dissolves==
No constituency — institutional distance from electoral politics
==Bicameral legitimacy== — elected by both Houses

items.title: Weaknesses
bullets: ==Partisan election vs impartial office== — no de-partisanisation convention
==LS predominance== in the electoral college; ==states don't vote== though he chairs the Council of States
Vague removal standard; broad procedural discretion without reasoned orders
==Art 68==: casual vacancy election ‘as soon as possible’ — ==no outer limit==

items: ==Way forward==: visible impartiality; ==reasoned public rulings==; codified removal-notice procedure; time-bound quasi-judicial decisions; ==consensus candidatures==; an indicative statutory vacancy timeline.

blocks.text: As the impartial custodian of legislative integrity, the Presiding Officer embodies the rule of law, fair deliberation and institutional balance — ensuring that both power and restraint co-exist within the House.
